title: Heat Early Warning System Coverage And Effectiveness In Low-Income Countries
summary: Build a verified workflow for mapping heat early warning system coverage and estimating mortality-reduction effectiveness in LMICs, with explicit documentation of mortality-data quality, climate-trend adjustment, and the distinction between system existence and demonstrated effectiveness.
why_it_matters: Heatwaves are the deadliest weather-related hazard globally, yet fewer than 40 LMICs have operational heat-health early warning systems. Where systems exist, systematic evaluation of mortality-reduction effectiveness is almost entirely absent — fewer than 10 countries have published pre- and post-warning-system mortality comparison data. The gap between nominal warning-system existence and verified effectiveness is the core measurement problem. Without verified effectiveness analysis, investment in heat early warning cannot be prioritized against competing climate-adaptation investments.

success_metrics.target: Every heat early warning system coverage claim links to WMO, IFRC, or national meteorological agency source with date, geographic scope, and operational status documentation.
success_metrics.validation_method: Schema validation and reviewer spot-checks against WMO and national meteorological agency documentation.

success_metrics.target: No mortality-reduction effectiveness claim is merged without documenting mortality-data quality, climate-trend adjustment, and the distinction between system existence and verified effectiveness.
success_metrics.validation_method: Red-team reviewer confirms every effectiveness claim has explicit data-quality and confound-control documentation.

success_metrics.target: Every effectiveness estimate documents whether it is derived from the analyzed LMIC context or transferred from high-income country studies with explicit transferability caveats.
success_metrics.validation_method: Reviewer confirms effectiveness estimate source and LMIC-applicability assessment.

review_policy.merge_gate: Schema validation, domain review, effectiveness-evaluation safety review, and replication for quantitative outputs.
